text.title: Tokio Async Service Patterns
text.summary: Async service methods: try_join! for parallel I/O, spawn_blocking for CPU work, structured error propagation.

rule.statement: Use `tokio::try_join!` for 2 or more independent async calls that can run in parallel. Sequential `.await` chains serialize operations that could be concurrent.
rule.rationale: `try_join!` starts all futures simultaneously and awaits both. Sequential `.await` runs them one at a time. For two 50ms DB queries, `try_join!` takes 50ms total; sequential takes 100ms.

rule.statement: Wrap CPU-bound operations (password hashing, image processing, crypto) in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`. Never call blocking functions directly on an async thread.
rule.rationale: Tokio's async thread pool is sized for I/O waiting. A 100ms CPU computation on an async thread stalls every other task scheduled on that worker thread, causing tail-latency spikes.

section.title: JoinHandle Management

rule.statement: Every `tokio::spawn` handle must be either awaited or explicitly dropped via `AbortHandle`. Orphaned handles leak memory and suppress errors silently.
rule.rationale: An unpolled `JoinHandle` keeps the spawned task alive indefinitely. The task's `Result` is dropped without being inspected, so panics and errors vanish without a trace.

section.reference: Reference async service with try_join!, spawn_blocking, and error propagation
